title: Designing a Safe LLM Application
concept: Combine input guardrails, aligned models, output filters, and user reporting loops to build a robust, safe product. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Monitoring LLM Apps.'

exercises.prompt: How do developers build a safe LLM application for the public?

options.text: Relying only on the AI model to behave perfectly.
Combining input guardrails, aligned models, and output filters.
Hoping that users will not write bad prompts.

exercises.explain: A safe application doesn't rely on just one safety check; it uses a multi-layered defense (guardrails, alignment, filters, and user feedback) to catch problems at every step.

cards.title: Keeping AI Safe
cards.body: Even the smartest AI models can make mistakes, hallucinate, or be tricked by clever prompts. If you are building an app for real users, how do you make sure the AI always behaves itself and never outputs anything harmful or inappropriate? You build a safety shield around it!

cards.title: Safe LLM Architecture
cards.body: A safe LLM application uses "defense-in-depth." This means combining multiple layers of protection: blocking bad prompts before they reach the model (input guardrails), choosing a model trained to be helpful and harmless (aligned models), checking the generated text before showing it to the user (output filters), and letting users flag issues (feedback loops).

cards.title: The Amusement Park Safety Net
cards.body: Think of building a safe LLM application like setting up an exclusive amusement park. You have a ticket gate at the entrance to check bags (input guardrails), well-trained staff inside the park (aligned models), a safety inspector checking the rides before people board (output filters), and a red emergency button for guests to report any hazards (user feedback loop). If one system fails, the others are there to keep everyone safe!

cards.title: The Homework Helper Bot
cards.body:
Imagine building a homework helper chatbot for kids. 
1. **Input Guardrail:** It blocks prompts like "How do I make a weapon?" 
2. **Aligned Model:** The AI is trained to politely decline inappropriate topics.
3. **Output Filter:** If the AI accidentally mentions a bad word, the filter censors it before the kid sees it.
4. **Reporting:** Parents can click a "Report" button if the AI gives an odd response.

cards.title: The Safe Model Myth
cards.body: Many people think that if you use a "safe" model like GPT-4 or Claude, your app is automatically 100% safe. This is a myth! Clever users can still bypass model safety using "jailbreaks." You always need external guardrails and filters to protect your users.

cards.title: One Shield vs. Complete Armor
cards.body: Compare a single-point safety setup with a multi-layered safe LLM application design.

right.text: Input guardrails + aligned model + output filters + user reporting loops working together.

left.text: Only relying on the LLM's built-in safety training. One jailbreak can bypass everything.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of an input guardrail in an LLM application?

options.text: To block harmful or malicious prompts before they reach the LLM
To make the LLM generate responses much faster
To automatically correct spelling mistakes in the user's prompt
To translate the prompt into multiple languages

exercises.explain: Input guardrails scan and block malicious or inappropriate prompts before they ever reach the LLM, saving computation time and preventing jailbreaks.

exercises.prompt: True or False: User reporting buttons are necessary because safety filters cannot catch 100% of bad outputs.
exercises.explain: User feedback loops (like thumbs up/down or flag buttons) are essential because automated filters aren't perfect and will occasionally miss bad outputs.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the safety workflow.
exercises.sentence: To catch bad responses before users see them, we use output _____.
exercises.explain: Output filters act as a final safety net, checking the AI's response before it is shown to the user.

exercises.prompt: Which system architecture represents a safe, production-ready LLM application?

options.text: Checks user prompts, uses an aligned AI model, and scans the final output before displaying it.
Sends raw user prompts directly to an unaligned model and shows the response immediately.
Rely only on a basic keyword filter on the user's screen with no backend checks.

exercises.explain: The first option shows a secure setup using input checks, an aligned model, and output filters. The second option has no safety measures at all, and the third relies on a single point of failure.

exercises.prompt: What is the most effective strategy for ensuring your LLM application is safe for public use?

options.text: Only filter the output, because users never write bad prompts.
Rely entirely on the LLM's built-in training without any extra guardrails.
Use multiple layers: input checks, an aligned model, output filters, and user feedback.
Disable safety filters to make the application respond faster.

exercises.explain: By combining checks at every stage—from the user's input to the model's inner alignment, the final output, and real-time user feedback—you create a defense-in-depth system that makes your LLM app safe for everyone. Next up: Monitoring LLM Apps.
